description: Detect vague, directionless, or evidence-free framing that expects the reader to fill in the blanks, accept claims on faith, or sympathize without justification.

rules.name: Open-Ended Claims / Dangling Assertions
rules.explanation: Plants a suspicion or implication without committing to a specific claim. The speaker avoids making a falsifiable statement — instead they 'just ask questions' or 'raise concerns', leaving the reader to construct the accusation themselves. The speaker gets the effect of an accusation without the accountability of making one.

rules.name: Meandering Thought / Directionless Padding
patterns: at the end of the day
when all is said and done
with respect to the fact that
the fact of the matter is
rules.explanation: Filler phrases that create the illusion of substance without advancing the argument. The text sounds authoritative but says nothing — remove these phrases and the paragraph loses no meaning. High density of these phrases signals the writer is padding space rather than making a point. Look for paragraphs where the connective tissue outweighs the actual content.

rules.name: Evidence-Free Assertions
rules.explanation: Asserts a claim as self-evident to avoid providing evidence. The stronger the assertion language ('obviously', 'clearly', 'without question'), the more likely the claim actually needs support. These phrases function as a substitute for proof — they signal 'believe this because I said it forcefully'. If the claim were truly obvious, the assertion word would be unnecessary.

rules.name: Self-Victimization Framing
patterns: they don't want you to know
they're afraid of what I have to say
I'm paying the price for speaking the truth
rules.explanation: Frames the speaker as a martyr or victim of persecution to preemptively discredit any criticism. If critics are 'persecutors', then criticism itself becomes evidence of a conspiracy rather than a response to the speaker's claims. The victimhood narrative makes the speaker untouchable — any pushback 'proves' the persecution. Look for persecution claims that appear right before or after a contested claim.

rules.name: Weasel Words / Hedge Stacking
rules.explanation: Attributes claims to anonymous, unverifiable sources to appear balanced while injecting unsubstantiated content. 'Some people say X' introduces X into the reader's mind without anyone taking responsibility for it. The writer can later deny endorsing it — 'I was just reporting what others say.' Look for unnamed plurals ('some', 'many', 'critics') serving as the sole source.

rules.name: Unfalsifiable / Kafka Trap Framing
patterns: the fact that you're defensive shows
if you don't see it you're part of the problem
only someone who is guilty would
you wouldn't be upset if it weren't true
rules.explanation: Constructs a claim where any response — agreement, disagreement, or silence — confirms the accusation. Agreeing proves the claim. Denying it proves you're defensive (therefore guilty). Saying nothing proves complicity. The claim becomes unfalsifiable — there is no possible response that counts as innocent. Look for framing where the accused literally cannot win.

rules.name: Implied Conclusion / Strategic Vagueness
patterns: take that for what it's worth
make of that what you will
rules.explanation: Implies a damning conclusion while explicitly declining to state it. 'I'm not saying X, but...' says X. 'Curious timing' implies conspiracy. The speaker gets the persuasive effect of the implication while maintaining plausible deniability — 'I never actually said that.' Look for rhetorical questions and conspiratorial framing that stop just short of a direct claim.
